id: De twee gezichten van Agentic AI wapen en schild__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0007
text:
Recent research by Palo Alto Networks, a US cyber security company, shows how profound this shift is. Researchers managed to simulate a full ransomware attack in just 25 minutes using Agentic AI — a hundred times faster than traditional methods. “The scenario they outline is disturbingly realistic,” says Matthijs Mons, cyber security specialist at ABN AMRO. “Criminals will develop agents that are tailored to targeted attacks.”
From science fiction to reality.

metadata.source_title: The two faces of Agentic AI: weapon and shield

metadata.author: Amad Khan
metadata.description: Criminals use AI to carry out lightning-fast attacks. The urgency is growing, but many organisations do not know where to start. Read more.
metadata.parent_text:
Recent research by Palo Alto Networks, a US cyber security company, shows how profound this shift is. Researchers managed to simulate a full ransomware attack in just 25 minutes using Agentic AI — a hundred times faster than traditional methods. “The scenario they outline is disturbingly realistic,” says Matthijs Mons, cyber security specialist at ABN AMRO. “Criminals will develop agents that are tailored to targeted attacks.”
From science fiction to reality. The process described by Palo Alto Networks illustrates the power of Agentic AI. Agents start by gathering public information about their targets via social media and corporate databases. Based on current data, they continually adapt their strategy and try to gain access to networks through personalised phishing attacks, LinkedIn messages or fake invitations to video conferences. Once inside, they analyse patterns to identify the most valuable systems and choose attack techniques that align seamlessly with their target’s vulnerabilities. “For many people this scenario sounds fictional, as if everything happens by itself,” says Mons. “But with traditional AI a human still had to perform at least an initial action. With Agentic AI, things now happen fully autonomously, without anyone being involved.

Researchers managed to simulate a full ransomware attack in just 25 minutes using Agentic AI — a hundred times faster than traditional methods. “The scenario they outline is disturbingly realistic,” says Matthijs Mons, cyber security specialist at ABN AMRO. “Criminals will develop agents that are tailored to targeted attacks.”
From science fiction to reality. The process described by Palo Alto Networks illustrates the power of Agentic AI. Agents start by gathering public information about their targets via social media and corporate databases. Based on current data, they continually adapt their strategy and try to gain access to networks through personalised phishing attacks, LinkedIn messages or fake invitations to video conferences. Once inside, they analyse patterns to identify the most valuable systems and choose attack techniques that align seamlessly with their target’s vulnerabilities. “For many people this scenario sounds fictional, as if everything happens by itself,” says Mons. “But with traditional AI a human still had to perform at least an initial action. With Agentic AI, things now happen fully autonomously, without anyone being involved.

text: They deploy simulations for their clients and learn from both the attacking “red teams” and the defending “blue teams”. This combined approach, also known as “purple AI”, enables organisations to learn from both perspectives and continuously improve their security.

metadata.chunk_id: De twee gezichten van Agentic AI wapen en schild__a9-hybrid-small-to-big__0025
metadata.source_slug: De twee gezichten van Agentic AI wapen en schild
metadata.source_title: The two faces of Agentic AI: weapon and shield
metadata.tags: Analyse, Technologie, Media & Telecom, AI, Cybersecurity, Personeelstekort
metadata.author: Amad Khan
metadata.description: Criminals use AI to carry out lightning-fast attacks. The urgency is growing, but many organisations do not know where to start. Read more.
metadata.parent_text:
They deploy simulations for their clients and learn from both the attacking “red teams” and the defending “blue teams”. This combined approach, also known as “purple AI”, enables organisations to learn from both perspectives and continuously improve their security. It is important to note that Agentic AI uses existing methods. It executes attacks faster than ever, but it does not devise new methods. So far, Agentic AI is mainly a leap in efficiency. “The major cyber security companies are investing heavily in AI,” says Mons. “But criminals are just as active. The romantic image of hackers as lonely boys in basements is completely outdated. Cyber attacks are carried out by professional organisations with specific roles focused on gaining access, building malware, installing malware, stealing data, negotiations, etc. They even have HR departments and call centres, with a solid business case based on the proceeds they generate from stolen data.”
The paradox of adoption. The use of Agentic AI within organisations — for both security and regular business processes — also creates new vulnerabilities. Hackers can, for example, try to manipulate the AI agents that already have access to systems. This leads to a striking paradox, revealed by a survey by SailPoint (a US technology company focused on identity and access management) among 353 cyber security professionals worldwide. Although 96 per cent of respondents consider agents to be a growing security threat, as many as 98 per cent of organisations plan to expand their use of Agentic AI in the coming year. The study shows that 82 per cent of companies already use agents, with more than half reporting that these have daily access to sensitive data. Nevertheless, only 44 per cent have implemented policies to protect these agents against external threats. Perhaps most alarming: nearly one in four companies reported that agents can be manipulated to reveal access credentials. “The use of this technology is a risk in itself,” warn Hans van Haarlem and Steven Ottenhoff of MMOX, a Dutch cyber security consultancy. They point to a crucial point that many entrepreneurs overlook. “In the past, attacking large companies took enormous time and resources.

metadata.author: Amad Khan
metadata.description: Criminals use AI to carry out lightning-fast attacks. The urgency is growing, but many organisations do not know where to start. Read more.
metadata.parent_text:
The experts at MMOX distinguish three essential factors. First, organisations must set clear guidelines for safe behaviour and provide user-friendly security tools. They warn: “Many tools wither on the vine because adoption lags.” Second, the urgency of cyber risks must be emphasised continuously. “This is not something for a single training session, but must be institutionalised. Discuss it monthly or quarterly during meetings, repetition is essential.” Third, organisations must understand the working environment. “Map which systems you have. Listen carefully to your employees. If they download software or extensions themselves because they are missing something in the official systems, you need to take that seriously. By understanding their needs and meeting them, you prevent them from seeking unsafe solutions. Make the topic open for discussion.”
Resilience rather than perfection. The central message from all experts is clear: perfect security does not exist. “The question is not whether you will be hacked, but when,” say the experts at MMOX. “The costs of attacks have become so low that every company is a potential target. We therefore advise clients: be prepared. Develop resilience.” Concretely, that means: ensure backups that actually work, develop an incident response system — a structured step-by-step plan to respond quickly and effectively to a cyber incident — and practise regularly with scenarios. “Simulate that you have been hacked,” they advise. “What will you do? How will you communicate?

The two faces of Agentic AI — as weapon and as shield — force organisations to rethink. The technology offers unprecedented opportunities for efficient security and scaling up cyber security teams. But that same power is also in the hands of criminals who are becoming more professional. The challenge is clear: companies must now invest in fundamental security, involve and prepare their employees, and realise that absolute security does not exist. Resilience and preparation are the new watchwords in an era where the question is not whether, but when you will be attacked.
